title: Access Dictionary Items
content:
### Accessing Dictionary Items

---

### Square Bracket Notation

```python
student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}
print(student["name"])  # "Alice"
print(student["age"])   # 25
```

> **Error:** Raises `KeyError` if key does not exist.

---

### `.get()` -- Safe Access

```python
student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}
print(student.get("name"))         # "Alice"
print(student.get("grade"))        # None (no error!)
print(student.get("grade", "N/A")) # "N/A" (default value)
```

---

### Get All Keys, Values, Items

```python
student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25, "grade": "A"}

print(student.keys())    # dict_keys(["name", "age", "grade"])
print(student.values())  # dict_values(["Alice", 25, "A"])
print(student.items())   # dict_items([("name", "Alice"), ("age", 25), ("grade", "A")])
```

---

### Check if Key Exists

```python
student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25}

print("name" in student)    # True
print("grade" in student)   # False
print("grade" not in student)  # True
```

---

### Loop Through Dictionary

```python
for key in student:
    print(key, student[key])

for key, value in student.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")
```

> **Best Practice:** Use `.get()` instead of `[]` when you are not sure a key exists. It avoids `KeyError`.
codeExample:
student = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25, "grade": "A"}

print("Name:", student["name"])
print("Grade:", student.get("grade"))
print("Missing:", student.get("missing", "not found"))

print("Keys:", list(student.keys()))
print("Values:", list(student.values()))
print("Items:", list(student.items()))

print("name" in student)

quiz.question: Which method safely accesses a dictionary key without raising an error?
